steps.title: MCQ_Endocrinology_Final.docx
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Diabetes
steps.text: Which test is considered the gold standard for diagnosing Type 1 Diabetes in a newly diagnosed child?
steps.rationale: Antibody tests are the gold standard for confirming a diagnosis of Type 1 Diabetes, especially at the tertiary care level.

steps.title: Diabetes
steps.text: Children diagnosed with Type 1 Diabetes should also be screened for which two autoimmune diseases?
steps.rationale: Thyroid disease and celiac disease are commonly associated autoimmune conditions in children with Type 1 Diabetes and should be screened at tertiary level.

steps.title: Diabetes
steps.text: What is the typical total daily insulin requirement for a prepubertal child with Type 1 Diabetes?
steps.rationale: Prepubertal children typically need 0.5–1.0 IU/kg/day of insulin, divided into multiple doses based on glucose and meals.

textChoices.text: Insulin is only used during illness

steps.title: Diabetes
steps.text: What should be done with insulin dosing in a child with Type 1 Diabetes who becomes ill?
steps.rationale: Insulin should not be stopped during illness. Instead, the dose is increased temporarily to manage the increased glucose needs during stress or infection.

textChoices.text: Stop insulin until the child recovers
Maintain the usual dose, but skip nighttime insulin
Only give insulin if blood sugar is above 250 mg/dL
Increase the insulin dose by 10–20% and taper after recovery

steps.title: Diabetes
steps.text: At tertiary level, what is a key initial management step when a child presents with signs of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)?
steps.rationale: Prompt rehydration and DKA treatment are critical steps in managing children with suspected ketoacidosis at tertiary level.

textChoices.text: Rehydrate the patient and treat for DKA

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: A child presents with DKA and is in shock. What is the correct initial fluid management step?
steps.rationale: Fluid resuscitation is the first step; 20 ml/kg bolus of 0.9% saline/RL over 20–30 minutes is recommended to restore circulation in shock/dehydration.

textChoices.text: Give 10 ml/kg of 0.45% saline over 1 hour
Give 20 ml/kg bolus of 0.9% saline or RL
Withhold fluids until blood sugar is corrected

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: When should insulin therapy be started in DKA management?
steps.rationale: Insulin must be started at least 1 hour after fluids to avoid sudden shifts in osmolarity and reduce cerebral edema risk.

textChoices.text: At the same time as fluid resuscitation
Only after blood glucose exceeds 300 mg/dL
At least 1 hour after starting fluid therapy

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: What is the recommended starting dose of insulin infusion in DKA management?
steps.rationale: Insulin infusion begins at 0.05 IU/kg/hour after fluid resuscitation.

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: Which of the following is a key sign of possible cerebral edema in a child with DKA?
steps.rationale: Cerebral edema often presents with headache, vomiting, decreased consciousness, and neurological signs.

textChoices.text: Headache, vomiting, and altered mental status

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: Which factor increases the risk of developing cerebral edema in children with DKA?
steps.rationale: Severe acidosis and elevated BUN are significant risk factors for cerebral edema in DKA.

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: When should hourly blood glucose checks begin during insulin therapy?
steps.rationale: Hourly blood glucose monitoring is critical after starting insulin to monitor drops and adjust dosing.

steps.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
steps.text: Once DKA resolves and subcutaneous insulin is administered, what should be done with the IV insulin?
steps.rationale: IV insulin is continued for 30 minutes after subcutaneous insulin to ensure overlap and prevent rebound hyperglycemia.

textChoices.text: Continue IV insulin for 30 minutes, then stop

steps.title: Thyroid Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following investigations is part of the initial assessment for thyroid dysfunction in a neonate?
steps.rationale: T4 and TSH assays are essential baseline investigations to assess thyroid function in neonates.

steps.title: Thyroid Disorders
steps.text: What is the recommended initial dose of levothyroxine for neonates (0–3 months of age)?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends a starting dose of 10–15 µg/kg/day of levothyroxine in infants to ensure effective treatment.

steps.title: Thyroid Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following may interfere with levothyroxine absorption in neonates?
steps.rationale: Iron, calcium supplements, and soy formula can impair absorption of levothyroxine and should be avoided during administration.

textChoices.text: Iron or calcium supplements and soy formula

steps.title: Thyroid Disorders
steps.text: What is the primary treatment goal in congenital hypothyroidism management?
steps.rationale: The target is to normalize T4 as rapidly as possible to ensure optimal growth and neurodevelopment.

textChoices.text: Achieve serum T4 in the upper half of the reference range
Maintain serum TSH below the detection limit
Delay treatment until growth retardation is observed
Use minimum dose to prevent side effects

steps.title: Thyroid Disorders
steps.text: For infants with congenital central hypothyroidism, what guides treatment response monitoring?
steps.rationale: In central hypothyroidism, TSH is unreliable. Serum free T4 should guide treatment.

steps.title: Thyroid Disorders
steps.text: How often should serum T4 and TSH be monitored after 6 months of life in children under treatment for congenital hypothyroidism?
steps.rationale: Between 6 months and 3 years of age, thyroid function should be monitored every 2 to 3 months.

steps.title: Acquired Hypothyroidism
steps.text: What is the recommended initial levothyroxine dose for a child aged 3 to 10 years with acquired hypothyroidism?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that for children aged 3 to 10 years, the appropriate levothyroxine dose is 3–5 µg/kg per day.

steps.title: Acquired Hypothyroidism
steps.text: When should serum TSH and free T4 be checked after initiating treatment for acquired hypothyroidism?
steps.rationale: Serum TSH and free T4 should be checked 6 to 8 weeks after initiation of levothyroxine treatment to evaluate response and guide dose adjustment.

steps.title: Acquired Hypothyroidism
steps.text: What is the main goal of levothyroxine therapy in acquired hypothyroidism?
steps.rationale: The treatment goal is to maintain TSH and free T4 within the normal reference range, ensuring proper metabolic control.

textChoices.text: To maintain TSH and free T4 in the normal reference range
To avoid the need for endocrinology referral
To prevent the need for further testing

steps.title: Acquired Hypothyroidism
steps.text: A child receiving levothyroxine presents with symptoms suggestive of hyperthyroidism. What is the appropriate next step?
steps.rationale: Thyroid function tests should be done if the patient develops symptoms suggestive of hypo- or hyperthyroidism, or after any dose change.

steps.title: Acquired Hypothyroidism
steps.text: Once treatment is confirmed and initiated, what is the appropriate next step in managing a child with acquired hypothyroidism?
steps.rationale: After diagnosis is confirmed and treatment is started, referral to an endocrinologist is recommended for ongoing care.

textChoices.text: Reduce levothyroxine to the lowest possible dose

steps.title: Hyperthyroidism
steps.text: Which of the following investigations should be performed at the tertiary level to confirm the diagnosis of hyperthyroidism?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that T4 and TSH assays are the required baseline investigations to confirm hyperthyroidism at the tertiary level.

textChoices.text: Full blood count and liver enzymes

steps.title: Hyperthyroidism
steps.text: According to the guidelines, what is the appropriate starting dose of carbimazole for treating hyperthyroidism at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends starting treatment with carbimazole 15 mg daily once the diagnosis is confirmed.

steps.title: Approach to disorders of sex development (Ambiguous Genitalia)
steps.text: Which of the following is a required component of initial management for a child with ambiguous genitalia at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes the importance of supportive care, including managing dehydration and hypoglycemia, as an urgent first step at the tertiary level.

textChoices.text: Delay treatment until investigations are complete
Provide supportive care, including correction of dehydration and hypoglycemia
Refer directly to a social worker

steps.title: Approach to disorders of sex development (Ambiguous Genitalia)
steps.text: In addition to supportive care, what should guide specific treatment in a child with ambiguous genitalia at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Specific treatment should be based on the underlying diagnosis, determined through thorough investigations at the tertiary level.

textChoices.text: Standard protocol used for all patients
The child’s gender as assigned at birth
The underlying disease identified through investigations
The preferences of the primary caregiver only

steps.title: Approach to disorders of sex development (Ambiguous Genitalia)
steps.text: Which of the following is required at the tertiary level of care for effective management of disorders of sex development (ambiguous genitalia)?
steps.rationale: The guideline highlights that tertiary-level care must involve a multidisciplinary team, including a pediatric endocrinologist, pediatrician, geneticist, urologist, gynecologist, psychologist, nurses, and a social worker.

textChoices.text: Referral to a community clinic for long-term care
Follow-up by a single general practitioner

steps.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
steps.text: What is a key biochemical finding suggestive of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) in newborns?
steps.rationale: Elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone is the hallmark biochemical abnormality in CAH.

steps.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
steps.text: Which of the following is part of supportive management in a neonate presenting with adrenal crisis due to suspected CAH?
steps.rationale: Immediate management of dehydration and hypoglycemia is essential during adrenal crisis.

textChoices.text: Delay treatment until diagnosis is confirmed
Withhold fluids until lab values return

steps.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
steps.text: What is the appropriate initial glucocorticoid therapy for a newborn with confirmed CAH?
steps.rationale: Hydrocortisone is the first-line steroid for CAH, given in multiple daily doses.

steps.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
steps.text: In addition to hydrocortisone, what other medications are routinely included in newborn CAH management?
steps.rationale: To manage mineralocorticoid deficiency and salt-wasting, fludrocortisone and sodium chloride are necessary.

steps.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
steps.text: Which of the following is not typically included in the initial work-up for suspected CAH in a tertiary setting?
steps.rationale: Amylase is not part of the CAH work-up; the rest are essential components.

steps.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
steps.text: When should an endocrinologist be consulted for a child with CAH?
steps.rationale: Early involvement of an endocrinologist ensures proper hormone replacement and monitoring.

textChoices.text: Once diagnosis is confirmed and treatment is started

steps.title: Puberty Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following is a key treatment responsibility at the tertiary level for a child with a puberty disorder?
steps.rationale: Tertiary-level care includes treating the underlying cause of the puberty disorder as part of a comprehensive management approach.

textChoices.text: Treat the underlying cause of the disorder
Refer only if the child reaches puberty late

steps.title: Puberty Disorders
steps.text: What is an important supportive intervention for families at the tertiary level when managing puberty disorders?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes providing psychosocial support to families, recognizing the emotional and social challenges that puberty disorders can cause.

steps.title: Puberty Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following medications may be used at the tertiary level to manage puberty disorders?
steps.rationale: Leuprolide acetate, a gonadotropin analogue, is specifically listed in the guideline as part of medical management for puberty disorders at the tertiary level.

steps.title: Puberty Disorders
steps.text: Which class of medications, including leuprolide acetate, is used at the tertiary level to manage puberty disorders by suppressing early or abnormal hormone activity?
steps.rationale: The guideline includes gonadotropin analogues such as leuprolide acetate as part of medical therapy to manage hormone-related puberty disorders at the tertiary level.

steps.title: Delayed Puberty
steps.text: What is a key focus of tertiary-level care in the management of delayed puberty?
steps.rationale: Tertiary-level care includes investigating and treating the underlying cause of delayed puberty as a core clinical responsibility.

textChoices.text: Investigate and treat the underlying cause
Refer to a primary care physician

steps.title: Delayed Puberty
steps.text: What medical treatment is recommended at the tertiary level for a female patient with delayed puberty?
steps.rationale: The guideline specifies that females with delayed puberty should be treated with Estrogen and Progestin, while males should receive Testosterone as part of tertiary-level medical management.

steps.title: Delayed Puberty
steps.text: Which of the following is an important supportive measure provided to families at the tertiary level when managing delayed puberty?
steps.rationale: The guideline highlights the importance of offering psychosocial support to families to help them cope with the social and emotional impact of delayed puberty.

steps.title: Overweight and Obesity in Children and Adolescents
steps.text: Which investigation should be included in the secondary-level assessment of an overweight child, beyond what is done at primary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline specifies that U&Es and LFTs should be conducted at the secondary level to evaluate for possible obesity-related complications.

textChoices.text: Urea & Electrolytes (U&Es) and Liver Function Tests (LFTs)

steps.title: Overweight and Obesity in Children and Adolescents
steps.text: Why is measuring blood pressure important in the evaluation of overweight children at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Hypertension is a known complication of childhood obesity. Blood pressure monitoring allows for early detection and management.

steps.title: Overweight and Obesity in Children and Adolescents
steps.text: Which of the following dietary recommendations should be emphasized when managing overweight children at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Reducing high-glycemic index foods is part of effective dietary management, helping prevent blood sugar spikes and excessive weight gain.

textChoices.text: Reduce intake of foods with a high glycemic index
Avoid all fats in the diet

steps.title: Overweight and Obesity in Children and Adolescents
steps.text: Why might referral from the secondary level to a higher level of care be important in managing overweight and obese children?
steps.rationale: Referral ensures that children with complex obesity receive comprehensive multidisciplinary care, including input from medical and psychosocial specialists.

textChoices.text: To admit the child for emergency weight loss treatment
To enable access to multidisciplinary care

steps.title: Growth Disorders
steps.text: What is the purpose of calculating mid-parental height during the assessment of a child with suspected growth disorder at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Mid-parental height is used to estimate the genetic target height, helping differentiate normal short stature from pathologic growth failure.

textChoices.text: To estimate the child’s expected genetic height range
To determine if the child is obese

steps.title: Growth Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following investigations should be included at the tertiary level to assess thyroid function in a child with suspected growth disorder?
steps.rationale: The guideline specifies TSH and free T4 as the appropriate tests to evaluate thyroid dysfunction, a common cause of impaired growth.

steps.title: Growth Disorders
steps.text: Why is it important to calculate height velocity in the evaluation of a child with a growth disorder at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Tracking height velocity helps identify subtle or progressive growth failure, which may not be apparent from a single height measurement.

textChoices.text: To confirm the child's pubertal stage
To determine the rate of growth over time and detect growth failure

steps.title: Growth Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following investigations is recommended at the tertiary level to assess for chronic inflammation or systemic disease affecting growth?
steps.rationale: The guideline includes FBC and ESR to detect anemia, infection, or chronic inflammation, all of which may contribute to poor growth.

textChoices.text: Full blood count (FBC) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)

steps.title: Growth Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following is used at the tertiary level to evaluate bone maturation in a child with suspected growth delay?
steps.rationale: Bone age X-ray is listed in the guideline as a key investigation at the tertiary level to evaluate growth potential and skeletal maturity.

steps.title: Tall Stature
steps.text: What is the purpose of calculating mid-parental height during the assessment of tall stature at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that plotting parents’ heights and calculating mid-parental height helps determine whether a child’s tall stature is consistent with genetic potential.

textChoices.text: To assess if the child's height is within their genetic target range

steps.title: Tall Stature
steps.text: Which of the following investigations is recommended at the tertiary level to assess skeletal maturity in a child with tall stature?
steps.rationale: Bone age X-ray is used to evaluate skeletal maturity, which helps in predicting adult height and guiding management decisions.

steps.title: Tall Stature
steps.text: What is the purpose of measuring thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and free thyroxine (T4) in a child with tall stature?
steps.rationale: The guideline includes TSH and free T4 to screen for thyroid dysfunction, such as hyperthyroidism, which can lead to increased growth velocity.

textChoices.text: To evaluate for thyroid disorders such as hyperthyroidism

steps.title: Tall Stature
steps.text: When should IgF-1 and IgFBP-3 be included in the work-up of a child with tall stature at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends using IgF-1 and IgFBP-3 only if available and appropriate, particularly when an endocrine cause of tall stature is suspected and further hormonal evaluation is needed.

textChoices.text: In all patients with tall stature
When available and clinically indicated to assess growth hormone activity
As a routine screening for thyroid dysfunction
